Joshua Levy
@ojoshe
A Rust Porting Playbook: 7 Techniques for Highly Tested Agent-Written Code
After a few weeks of experimentation, I’m excited to share something new: the Rust Porting Playbook, a system of documents, guidelines, and tools for 100% agentic porting of Python codebases to Rust. In this post, I’ll talk about a practical example of porting a project to Rust. But this is really about more than porting: it is about 7 general techniques I’ve found are effective for highly tested, high-quality agentic coding. They work for porting, but the same techniques can be applied to many agent coding tasks.
I'm pleased to release everything here as open source. There are four GitHub repos (all links in replies below):
  • the rust-porting-playbook, which is a bundle of 19 documents (~190 pages) of reference, guideline, and process docs that guide agents in autonomously porting code to Rust
  • the case study of flowmark-rs, a 100% agent-written port to Rust of a full-featured Python Markdown formatter (you or your agent can try `uvx flowmark-rs --help`)
  • tryscript, simple but effective golden testing tool for more maintainable, deeper test coverage of CLI apps (`npx tryscript --help`)
  • tbd, a Beads-style agentic issue tracker that helps scale agents to hundreds of subtasks and hours of autonomous coding (`npx get-tbd@latest --help`)
There is quite a bit here. You might want to bookmark this if you can’t get through it in one sitting. This is my first long-form post here, so I’d appreciate if you share or let me know if you find it interesting.

How Good are Agents at Porting?

Porting code is a great challenge for agents. It’s a well-defined problem. Perhaps, if agents are getting better at coding so quickly, porting will soon be trivially easy?
As usual, the devil is in the details. Anyone can say to a coding agent: “please port this project to Rust.” Basic functionality will be written quickly—but then you hit harder parts.
Over the past few months, as coding agents have rapidly improved, I’ve tried this a few times as a way to measure the strength of coding agents.
Until now, I’ve found there are invariably places agents (just like human engineers) get stuck in messy details. There are three common failure modes when a coding task is too hard for an agent:
  1. It spins for hours, often repeating or forgetting its work because the debugging cycle uses up the context window
  2. It moves the goalposts, telling you it has completed the task (even when the new code does not meet the spec you gave it, such as feature parity)
  3. It gives up and tells you some part of the problem is impossible (for example, because a library has a limitation or bug)
These are partly issues with the coding agent’s abilities. But they are also inadequacies with the context and harness you’ve given the agent.
For example, for a long time, I’ve wanted a fast, native-binary version of Flowmark, a Markdown auto-formatter I maintain in Python. But the dozens of subtle differences in the Markdown parsing libraries in Rust (comrak) vs Python (marko) was too much for Claude Opus to achieve exact parity without human assistance.
However, now, with agents using new models like Opus 4.6 or GPT-5.3 Codex and tools and tricks like the ones I’ll talk about next, I believe fully automated porting is in reach for many projects. Especially for CLI apps, a few hours of agent work can give native binaries with exact feature parity and 10x to 100x the speed of the original. I’ve tested this for Python and expect it to work similarly for TypeScript.
Even better, the port is easily auto-synced: future changes to the source (Python) version flow downstream to target (Rust) with almost no human effort.
Critically, to support all this the agent-written testing must be extensive and transparent, so you (and reviewing agents) can gain confidence that the port is high fidelity. The era of auto-ported multi-language software is here.

Seven Techniques for Better Agentic Coding

The playbook uses 7 general agentic coding techniques that I’ve found especially powerful:
  1. Knowledge curation: Good context engineering requires high-quality, dense, relevant knowledge. It pays to build reusable, reliable background documents with best practices and pitfalls related to source and target languages, libraries, and tooling. Agents sometimes figure this out, but having pre-curated knowledge significantly raises the quality, reliability, and speed of porting. The rust-porting-playbook has ~200 pages of Rust best practices across its guidelines/, references/, playbooks/, and case-studies/ directories.
  2. Golden testing: This is an alternative to classic unit and integration testing that offers transparency and readability to both agents and humans. To help with this, I also built tryscript, an agent-friendly CLI tool that lets agents write golden tests in plain Markdown for CLI-based apps (more on both below).
  3. Test backfilling: Assuming a given Python project has good tests is not realistic. Very few projects have truly thorough test coverage. But once you have a golden testing methodology, it’s straightforward for an agent to backfill extensive golden tests and iterate until coverage is thorough. This could uncover bugs!
  4. Test mapping: Meta-tests that systematically map and automate parity checks across the two implementations. The beauty of golden testing is that the tests across the ports are actually the same! So CLI tests written in tryscript’s Markdown format can be reused between Python and Rust.
  5. Codified principles: Without explicit engineering principles in context, agents drift: silently reducing scope, skipping hard bugs, or “moving the goalposts” on tests. A principles doc fixes this.
  6. Task management: Agents struggle to manage hundreds of sub-tasks on their own. Git-native issue tracking (beads) helps enormously (more below).
  7. Meta-process improvement: The playbook improves itself through a structured case study feedback loop (more below).
The key point: with all process docs in the playbook covering each of the aspects above, the whole process itself can be automated, including the playbook self-improvement process.

What are the Limitations?

Before I give more details, let’s talk about the limitations. I’ve used the playbook to port just three projects so far. There are caveats:
  • The source project does not need to have perfect test coverage initially, but it should be amenable to testing via tests, especially golden testing (more on this below).
  • Porting works best if there are roughly equivalent libraries in both ecosystems. Most common Python libraries (CLI parsing, HTTP, regex, serialization, async) map cleanly to mature Rust crates. But some areas of deep learning have notable gaps, e.g. Rust has PyTorch bindings (tch-rs) and native frameworks like Burn, but the surrounding ecosystem still is not fully covered in Rust.
  • Although I’ve used Python for a long time, I’m new to Rust. I’d greatly appreciate thoughts from experienced Rust engineers on the results here.
Nonetheless, I think this approach is interesting because:
  • This whole methodology is self-improving. Some programs are hard to port, but each time we push past a tricky part, the playbook gets better.
  • This covers Python-to-Rust but it should extend well to TypeScript-to-Rust. (I'm testing this next!)
  • Even if you’re not porting code to Rust, the playbook here illustrates several novel techniques I’ve not seen elsewhere. These do apply to other kinds of agent coding.

An Example Rust Port

Let’s first look at the results of porting Flowmark. Then I’ll walk through details of each of the seven techniques. The flowmark-rs port was written entirely by Claude Opus 4.6, with a final pass on PRs by GPT-5.3 Codex Extra High. It took a few multi-hour sessions. I did give nudges or hand-offs between sessions but everything was completely completely handled by Claude. For some of the planning, Claude used sub-agents but for most of it, I just let it work through serially, unattended, with the help of specs and beads it had written. My job was not to tell it what to do step by step but to make sure the playbook was offering the needed structure. I also helped Claude with things that needed my authorization (like publishing to Homebrew, PyPI, and as a Crate). Here is a summary of the phases in the port process and basic stats, as reported by Claude using the playbook.
A summary report by Claude produced as part of the port process for Flowmark.


Python vs Rust Performance

So is the effort worth it to have a Rust port of this program?
After it was done, I ask Claude to benchmark performance of Flowmark. Performance was good, but there was room for improvement compared to other Rust-based formatters, particularly dprint, another Rust-based formatter.
Having come this far, I couldn’t help but want more. So I asked both Claude and Codex to suggest ways to improve efficiency to see if we could make this the fastest tool available. Based on the answers I suggested it (1) use multiple cores when running on many files and (2) add a persisted file cache to avoid reformatting files that have not changed. That worked well! Note these technically go beyond porting as they were not features in the original Python.
The current Rust code takes 0.73s to format ~1000 Markdown docs, which is about a 60x speed improvement over the Python version. With the cache in use, it takes just 0.023s to check all ~1000 files. I now use flowmark-rs every day in projects to auto-format all Markdown documents. I suggest using it as your format-on-save Markdown formatter in VS Code or Cursor. It feels instant.
Performance comparison of flowmark-rs vs flowmark-py showing a 60x+ speedup with the port to Rust.
Now let’s walk through each technique used.
The overall playbook workflow for Phases 1–6 (as drawn by Claude).

Knowledge Curation

Agents can figure out many things on their own, but pre-curated knowledge and guidelines dramatically reduces slow web searches and common confusions that suck up tokens and waste context.
Agents use these 19 docs (~190 pages) to guide the porting process:
  • Guidelines (7 docs, ~60 pages) are compact rules: porting principles and anti-patterns (the “north star” demanding exact parity and forbidding things like changing scope or skipping tests), test coverage requirements, and acceptance criteria.
  • References (5 docs, ~70 pages) are lookup tables and pattern catalogs: an exhaustive Python-to-Rust construct mapping (types, collections, error handling, async), CLI app patterns (clap, tracing, error handling), cross-language test mapping with CI enforcement, and distribution best practices.
  • Playbooks (4 playbooks + 3 templates, ~60 pages) are step-by-step process guides and checklists for the end-to-end port. A key insight: ~50% of effort goes to library workarounds and cross-validation, not the initial implementation.
It took me a while to assemble and review these docs using Claude, but once you have them, they are reusable. Each doc is small enough to fit into context well at an appropriate time. Claude and Codex are now quite good at consulting reference docs like this. (My tbd tool discussed below also has a skill to auto-inject knowledge docs like these.)

Golden Testing

Golden testing is kind of a secret weapon in agentic coding. In my opinion, it's always been one of the most useful types of testing. The idea is to capture the behavior of a program—actual inputs, outputs, and intermediate states—then serialize it to a readable file, save that session trace, and diff it later.
For some reason, golden testing is not well known or appreciated by many engineers. Perhaps it’s because no one has popularized a good name for it. Some have called it “snapshot testing” or “characterization testing,” but I think “golden testing” is a better description. It is more than snapshotting or characterizing program outputs: we are capturing a complete log of behavior and then comparing it with a “golden” session log.
Golden testing is more relevant than ever for agentic coding. Unit tests, integration tests, and even end-to-end tests tend to verify narrow expectations. In contrast, golden tests contextually reveal broad state, confirming expected results while simultaneously making unexpected changes obvious.
When you modify one thing and something else unexpectedly changes, a diff on what changed in the captured session reveals it immediately in the context of the use case of the test session. And it does this with lower maintenance burden than equivalent unit and integration tests.
Golden testing can be done for many kinds of code and even UIs and web applications, if you define an appropriate clean and diff-able serialization for sessions. For CLIs, golden testing is ideal.

Golden Testing CLIs with Tryscript

Let's give a concrete example using tryscript, a tool specifically built for CLI golden testing. It runs test scripts, which are simply Markdown files that include shell commands. It then captures stdout and stderr and compares it with the pre-filled values already in the script document. You check the script into Git. Then subsequent runs compare actual outputs against expected outputs. An example:
## Wrap long lines You can document a test like this: Let's see how flowmark handles narrow wrap widths! ```console $ echo "This is a paragraph that has been wrapped so that it reads well in a terminal or diff view." | flowmark --width 40 - This is a paragraph that has been wrapped so that it reads well in a terminal or diff view. ? 0 ``` ## Error handling Now let's try an error. ```console $ flowmark --width abc error: invalid value 'abc' for '--width <WIDTH>': invalid digit found in string For more information, try '--help'. ? 2 ```
Each test block has a heading, optional documentation, a command, the expected output, and the exit code (? 0 or ? 2). One detail: certain output values like IDs or timestamps can be different in every run. So you need a mechanism golden testing is only check the “stable” outputs. A handy way to do this is with pattern-based elision. Tryscript supports regex patterns for normalizing timestamps and IDs ([PATTERN]), line elisions (... for zero or more lines, [..] for any text on a single line), sandbox isolation, setup blocks, and environment variables, all in plain Markdown that humans and agents can both understand readily.
This design is ideal for agent testing and porting:
  1. Tests are the spec. You can have agents write specs and review the actual behavior exhaustively.
  2. Tests show behavior, not intent. Unit tests usually verify a developer (or agent’s) expectations. Golden tests capture the actual input and output.
  3. Tests are shared across languages. The tests are simply tryscript-format Markdown containing shell commands. So they are the same for both source and target language.
  4. Agents are great at creating and understanding golden tests. Writing golden tests is mechanical: an agent runs the test in “update” mode, which records the outputs, reviews and checks in the result. An agent can generate hundreds of golden tests, review them, commit.
  5. Humans can read golden tests. Reviewing a golden test is much easier than a long code file with integration tests. The behavior is shown in context of a real run.
In the case of Flowmark, by using golden testing, the agents caught many bugs that unit tests would have missed, such as mode-specific parity issues and systematic differences between the Python parser (marko) and Rust parser (comrak).

Test Backfilling

Most real-world projects don’t have thorough test coverage. But with a golden testing framework like tryscript, backfilling coverage is easy for agents: the agent writes a script showing all functionality, then runs the test script in update mode. This executes tool for every case in the session and records the outputs.
The agent reviews and commits the results. This can be done quickly and systematically before the real porting begins, giving the Rust implementation a precise spec and full tests to work against.
The playbook’s python-to-rust-test-coverage-playbook.md documents this process in detail.

Test Mapping

Test count alone is misleading. A Rust port can have hundreds of tests while still missing coverage for critical behaviors.
You can ask an agent to rewrite tests, but how do you know it did? How does the agent know got them all?
Test mapping addresses this by creating a formal, traceable link from every Python test to its Rust equivalent(s). In the playbook, this is a YAML manifest:
- python_file: tests/test_alerts.py python_function: test_alert_after_heading status: mapped rust_file: tests/test_alerts.rs rust_function: test_alert_after_heading
Every Python test gets a status: mapped (has a Rust equivalent), partial (one Python test split into multiple Rust tests), excluded (intentionally not ported, with a documented reason), or missing (not yet ported).
The process has three parts:
  1. Discovery: Auto-scan Python tests (via AST parsing) and Rust tests (via cargo test --list) to generate inventories of all tests on both sides.
  2. Mapping: Link each Python test to its Rust counterpart(s). If a single Python test with many assertions becomes multiple focused Rust tests, the mapping tracks all of them explicitly.
  3. Meta-test in CI: A validation check runs on every push, confirming the mapping is complete: no unmapped Python tests, no dangling references. This prevents silent coverage drift as the port evolves.
Golden tests (tryscript files) are shared directly between the two implementations, so they don’t need additional mapping. The mapping tracks unit and integration tests, where the Python and Rust implementations naturally diverge in structure.
In the flowmark port, this produced 292 Python tests mapped to 442 Rust tests, with 100% mapping coverage enforced in CI.

Codified Principles

A pitfall I found when trying earlier versions of the porting playbook was that the tests would be correct, and the mapping correct.
The agent would run for hours and tell me it was done. But then I'd discover that during the implementation process, Claude Opus had “moved the goalposts.” It might have changed the scope of the porting spec or quietly skipped some tests. Other times it might tell me the port was impossible becuase libraries were “incompatible.”
There was a simple fix to this: a short document codifying engineering principles and anti-patterns for the porting process. Having these explicitly in the agent’s context meant it would constantly remember its job was exact parity, not “close enough.”
Putting principles and anti-patterns in the same doc works well: the principles say what to do, and the anti-patterns give concrete examples of what not to do. Agents respond well to both positive and negative examples: just saying “ensure exact parity” is less effective than also specifying “NEVER disable failing tests.”
These are in porting-principles-and-antipatterns.md in the playbook repo.

Task Management

Agents now easily handle 5 or even 10 items in a to-do list, but longer spec-driven development and porting requires tracking dozens of steps. Agents easily lose track, repeat work, or lose task context between sessions.
Agent-friendly issue tracking solves this. Beads, created by Steve Yegge, are a remarkably effective way to scale an agent’s capacity to hundreds of structured, trackable issues that persist in Git across sessions. With beads, you can leave an agent running for hours.
I recommend tbd (“To Be Done”), my own TypeScript port of Beads. I find tbd is more reliable: it uses simple Markdown for each bead, which avoids many of the merge conflicts and complexity of the original Beads’ JSONL format. (It also offers additional features for spec-driven planning, self-injection of engineering guidelines, and other shortcuts like code review and PR creation—but those are less essential for porting tasks so I'll write more about that in a future post. GitHub link below.)
During the port, agents created and closed 235 beads.
A few of the 235 beads used by the agent during the port.

Meta-Process Improvement

There’s likely still a long way to go to make this work smoothly for really complex applications. But I’ve gone through it about 3 times and it’s improving.
Every port conducted using the playbook is a case study, and every case study generates observations that flow back into the playbook.
As the final phase of the playbook, an agent reviews the case study and triages observations into categories: FIX (factual error), ADD (missing guidance), CLARIFY (ambiguous), GENERALIZE (too project-specific), or VALIDATE (confirmed correct).
This is documented in meta-improving-this-playbook.md in the _meta/ directory.
What do you think? Even if you’re not porting a whole program, I hope some of these techniques are useful! If you've made it this far, do let me know what you think and what works or doesn't work.
Josh
